title: An Explanation for the Mayor and the People!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source was used to restore a damaged Tamil phrase, a letter date and the complete English government-order quotation. Repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: All newspapers, including *Dina Thanthi*, have reported that Chennai Mayor Saidai Duraisamy lashed out at a Corporation Council meeting: “The OSR land, the open space within Arivalayam, has still not been handed over to the Corporation through a gift deed. If you possess any sense of shame or honour, tell your leadership to place the land in the Corporation's custody.”

I have known the Mayor for many years. I did not expect him to stoop to speaking at this level. Nevertheless, to prevent Chennai's residents from becoming confused after reading his remarks, I have been left with no choice but to offer this explanation. Everyone knows how many times this open space within Anna Arivalayam has been discussed and how often the DMK has responded. Yet once every six months, the AIADMK continues to repeat the accusation.

In his own speech, Mayor Saidai Duraisamy said that during the previous government a special concession was granted for the OSR land within Arivalayam, a government order stated that no gift deed need be registered, and a separate resolution was adopted by the Corporation. Having said this himself, what does it mean for him then to claim that the land was not handed over to the Corporation through a gift deed?

When planning permission was obtained for the Anna Arivalayam building in 1988, the DMK Charitable Trust petitioned to maintain the approximately ten per cent Open Space Reservation land itself. The Tamil Nadu government accepted the petition and granted planning permission on condition that the OSR site be maintained as Anna Park, issuing a government order accordingly.

Just as the DMK Trust was granted an exemption, a government order was issued in 1988 permitting the Congress Trust to maintain the OSR land in front of its Kamarajar Arangam. It is noteworthy that the present accusation says nothing about the Congress Trust.

Anna Arivalayam has a history. It was built on behalf of the DMK Charitable Trust. Formal permission to construct it was obtained on 11 August 1980. After work began, shortages of cement and water, along with power cuts, caused some delay and prevented rapid completion. Beside the building, we also began constructing an ordinary shed to store the necessary materials and tools.

Then, suddenly, under the AIADMK government, certain MMDA officials inspected the building on 28 October 1985 and sent a letter on 30 October asking why the work had not been completed within the stipulated period and directing that it be stopped immediately. In our reply dated 2 November 1985, we said that steps would be taken to renew the permission and obtain permission for the adjoining shed. In its reply of 5 November, the MMDA said that unless it received confirmation by 12 November that construction had stopped, it would take stern action. In our letter of 7 November, we confirmed that the work had been stopped. In another letter written the same day, we requested renewal of the permission so that construction could continue.

In a letter dated 19 December 1985, the MMDA rejected our request, stating that adequate setback space and the required ten per cent open space had not been provided. A note beneath the letter said that an appeal could be made to the government within two months if necessary. We replied immediately that the defects cited would be rectified, six metres of space would be set aside, and the land reserved for an eye-care camp would instead be assigned to the open-space requirement. In its letter of 7 January 1986, the MMDA stipulated that an architect be appointed to supervise construction, progress reports be submitted every three months, trees be planted, and notification be given when the work was completed. It further stated that the OSR land must be conveyed and registered in the name of the Commissioner of the Madras Corporation through a registered gift deed, that Rs. 50,200 must be paid within ten days, and that building permission could be granted only thereafter.
In our letter dated 9 January 1986, we accepted the conditions and said that a cheque for Rs. 50,200 was being sent. We also requested:

“When we first applied to the government for permission in 1980, the government granted it with the welfare activities of our Trust in mind. At that time, no issue arose regarding allocation of this open space. We are now prepared, in accordance with the MMDA's view, to set aside the open space for public use under the name ‘Arignar Anna Park.’ Nevertheless, considering the important benevolent objectives of the Trust, it should be allowed to retain responsibility for managing this open space, the condition requiring its donation to the Corporation through a deed should not be insisted upon, and permission should be granted without delay.”

Besides writing to the MMDA, on 10 January 1986 we wrote all the details to the government and requested early permission to resume construction. On behalf of the party, we sent Anbil Dharmalingam and T.R. Baalu to the Housing Minister, who said a decision would soon be taken. Comrade Manickam, then State secretary of the Communist Party of India, suggested writing directly to the Chief Minister. Accordingly, on 5 March 1986, I wrote to our friend M.G.R., then Chief Minister. In his immediate reply, M.G.R. said that he would consider my request sympathetically and take appropriate action. I sent reminder letters to the Chief Minister on 19 and 22 March and spoke in the Legislative Council. No action was taken.
Thereafter, during Governor's Rule, the government order issued on 5 August 1988 stated clearly:

“The Government, after careful consideration of the appeal received from the Chairman and Managing Trustee, D.M.K. Charitable Trust in the reference first read above and the remarks offered by the Member Secretary, M.M.D.A., in the matter in his letter have decided to allow the appeal and to exempt the construction of office, Kalyanamandapam and Anathai Illam by the D.M.K. Charitable Trust from the provisions of rule 19 (b) (ii) of Development Control Rules relating to provision of 10% of the site extent as O.S.R., subject to the condition that the Trust should maintain the Open Space Reservation as Aringar Anna Recreation Park for the public and that no construction should be made in future at the Open Space Reservation place.”
Thus, the exemption for this site was granted by government order during Governor's Rule itself. The order was also published in the Tamil Nadu Government Gazette on 7 September 1988.

Even after that, in 2004 under the AIADMK government, the government wrote to the Trust saying that the Corporation Commissioner had reported that the open space was not being maintained properly and asked for an explanation. The Trust sent a detailed reply on 4 July 2004 and simultaneously filed a writ petition in the Madras High Court. The judge considered the DMK Trust's objection and directed the government to take action upon it.

In its reply dated 7 December 2007, the government stated that the MMDA Chief Planner and the Corporation's Chief Engineer had inspected the open-space park on 2 November 2007 and reported that it was being properly maintained as a park in accordance with the rules, with no violation. On that basis, the government closed the notice seeking an explanation, abandoned further action on it, and clearly directed that the site continue to be maintained as open space without any construction.

Every one of these steps was taken properly, and the documents and government orders exist. In these circumstances, what justice is there in the Mayor of a Corporation speaking in this manner? I hope that at least now he will stop making such remarks.

Newspapers continually report one problem after another in the Chennai Corporation. In an interview given last week to *Junior Vikatan*, former Mayor Thambi Ma. Subramanian said that the condition of Chennai Corporation could be understood simply by looking at its streets. He raised a succession of questions: about the nine flyovers built under the DMK government to reduce traffic congestion; the improvement in Corporation school performance from 50 to 80 per cent while the DMK was responsible; the creation of new parks; the recovery of Corporation properties worth Rs. 4,000 crore; and the conversion of Corporation-owned OSR lands into public parks. He also asked why OSR lands now due to the Corporation were instead being returned to the very companies concerned; why the DMK government paid Rs. 642 to remove one tonne of refuse while a Hyderabad company is now paid Rs. 1,542; and where the difference goes.

I would have been pleased had the Mayor answered all these questions. Instead, he acts and speaks in the hope of continuing to earn the praise of those already hostile towards me. I wish to tell him that such conduct does no credit to the office he holds.
Affectionately,
M.K.
30 December 2013
